data.title: Ford Heads Into Q1 With Modest Revenue Growth And Deepening EV Losses
data.description:
Ford Motor Co. (F) is set to report first quarter 2026 earnings on April 29, with Visible Alpha consensus estimates pointing to modest revenue growth alongside flat volumes and a sharp pullback in electric vehicles.

Consensus expectations show automotive revenue to rise 2.3% year-on-year to $38.3 billion in Q1, even as unit sales are expected to hold broadly flat at around 972,000 vehicles. The muted growth reflects a wider cooling in the US auto market, where high borrowing costs and elevated vehicle prices continue to constrain demand.

Within Ford’s divisional structure, the contrast is increasingly stark. The traditional internal combustion business, Ford Blue, is expected to deliver ~593,000 units, with revenue edging up 2.2% to $21.5 billion.

Segment operating income is expected to climb sharply to $493 million from $96 million a year earlier, helped by easing supply chain constraints following disruption from the 2025 fire at the Novelis aluminum plant, which significantly disrupted production, along with a stronger focus on high-margin products, and reduced manufacturing costs.

Ford Pro, the group’s commercial vehicle arm and a key profit driver, is forecast to generate $15.4 billion in revenue, up 1.1%. However, operating profit is seen slipping marginally to $1.3 bn, down 1% from the prior year, indicating some normalization after a period of strong post-pandemic demand.

By contrast, the electric vehicle unit, Model e, continues to retrench. Analysts expect volumes to fall 12% year-on-year to just 27,000 units, with revenue dropping 19% to roughly $1 billion. Operating loss is also projected to widen sharply by 14% to $968 million.

The decline mirrors a broader reversal in US EV demand following the expiry of federal tax credits and high price points, which have pushed many consumers back towards cheaper combustion and hybrid models. Ford is already recalibrating its EV strategy, scaling back investment and shifting focus towards more profitable segments.

data.title: Maritime Chokepoints Tighten, Global Supply Chain Risk Rises
data.description:
How is shipping rerouting impacting global trade?

Rerouting from the Strait of Hormuz is increasing pressure on secondary maritime chokepoints, including the Panama Canal and Malacca Strait. This is creating geographically diffuse supply chain risk, where network congestion—rather than a single point of failure—is becoming the primary channel for global trade disruption.

Weather-related events and competition for transit during peak shipping season are expected to amplify these risks in the coming months.

Why is network congestion a rising concern for supply chains?

As peak shipping season approaches, the spillover effects from disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz are no longer contained. Increased tanker traffic and weather-driven constraints are tightening capacity at the Panama Canal and Malacca Strait. This dynamic means simple rerouting strategies are becoming less effective, and firms must now account for broader, interconnected network risks that extend beyond a single geopolitical flashpoint.

What is the current state of maritime chokepoints?

Pressure building beyond Hormuz

As peak season approaches, pressure is building across global maritime chokepoints beyond the Strait of Hormuz.

Panama Canal traffic shifts

Panama Canal traffic rose sharply in March, with more modest growth through the Malacca Strait. The canal is operating at full capacity, and traffic composition has shifted. Tankers accounted for more than one-third of transits in March, overtaking container ships, amid stronger demand for US LNG linked to the Iran conflict.

Inventory and weather risks

Although Panama Canal current water levels support higher transit volumes, depleted global oil and gas inventories and rising tanker flows raise congestion risks into peak season. These risks are amplified by a high probability of El Niño conditions emerging between May and July, which could reduce rainfall in Central America and constrain Panama Canal transit slots months after.

Increased regional traffic

Traffic through the Malacca Strait, the Suez Canal and the Bab al-Mandeb Strait also increased month-over-month in March.

How will weather patterns affect shipping?

Tropical storms as bottlenecks

In many regards, tropical storms can be considered as another form of shipping bottleneck, adding to the uncertainty caused by geopolitics and other weather systems such as El Niño. Such impacts hit unevenly, however, particularly as El Niño tends to coincide with weaker hurricane seasons in the North Atlantic while increasing storms and rainfall volatility elsewhere.

Atlantic storm season and peak season overlap

The peak storm season in the North Atlantic typically runs from June onward each year, with a peak in September. That coincides with arrivals of peak-season consumer goods into US East Coast ports, which are typically reached in September each year, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. Caribbean ports are also heavily exposed, with storms causing disruptions for supply chains more broadly, as seen with Hurricane Melissa in 2025, which heavily impacted Jamaica.

2026 storm season forecast

The North Atlantic is expected to experience 13 named tropical storms in 2026, according to forecasts from Colorado State University, including six hurricanes. That is slightly below the 14.4 storm average from 1991 to 2020 and well below the 20.4 average since 2020 but only in line with 2025’s 13 storms.

What is the outlook for maritime trade?

Near-term congestion outlook

Congestion risks at secondary chokepoints will rise over the next two to three months, as tanker traffic linked to LNG and refined fuels competes for transit with container shipping during peak season.

Panama Canal as a binding constraint

The Panama Canal is likely to remain a binding constraint, with limited spare capacity amplifying the impact of any weather-related disruptions later in the year.

Network congestion as the new norm

Shipping delays are more likely to propagate through network congestion rather than a single point of closure, extending disruption beyond the Middle East.

Understanding the Impact of Widening Chokepoint Pressures

What is causing increased pressure on the Panama Canal?

According to the analysis, a sharp rise in traffic in March has pushed the canal to full capacity. This is driven by a shift in traffic composition, where increased demand for US LNG has led to tankers accounting for over one-third of transits, overtaking container ships.

How do weather events add to supply chain risk?

Weather introduces significant uncertainty. The analysis notes a high probability of El Niño conditions, which could reduce rainfall and constrain transit slots at the Panama Canal. Separately, the North Atlantic tropical storm season, which peaks in September, coincides with peak-season arrivals on the US East Coast and can cause major disruptions, as seen with past hurricanes.

Why is simple rerouting no longer an effective strategy?

As the article states, supply chain risk is becoming "geographically diffuse." With pressure building on secondary chokepoints like the Panama and Malacca Straits simultaneously, the problem is shifting from a single point of closure to widespread network congestion. This makes it difficult to find alternative routes that are not also experiencing delays or capacity constraints.

data.title: Virtus KAR International Small-Mid Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

International small- and mid-cap stocks, as measured by the benchmark MSCI All Country World Ex USA Small Mid Cap Index, advanced 0.35% during the first quarter. AI-driven momentum remained a key market force, exhibiting continued strength in areas such as semiconductor capital equipment, and driving sector-wide selloffs in areas like software and IT services, which the market views as having a high risk of disruption. The outbreak of the war in Iran pushed oil prices sharply higher, making energy the standout sector of the quarter and reinforcing strength in defense-oriented industries.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR International Small-Mid Cap Fund (VISAX) returned -6.32% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the MSCI All Country World Ex USA Small Mid Cap Index's 0.35% return. Stock selection and an overweight position in Europe ex-UK, combined with stock selection and an underweight position in Japan, detracted from performance. An overweight position in Latin America and an underweight position in the United States contributed positively to performance.

Caixa Seguridade and VAT Group (VACNY) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • > Caixa Seguridade is one of the largest insurance brokers in Brazil. The company has attractive joint venture interests in the entities that underwrite the policies Caixa distributes. The company reported modest quarterly results, but a strong rally in Brazilian equities brought positive sentiment to share price performance.
  • > VAT, based in Switzerland, is one of the world's largest manufacturers of vacuum valves. The shares outperformed on the back of strong order momentum from semiconductor equipment customers.

MonotaRO and FinecoBank were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • > MonotaRO, headquartered in Japan, is a distributor of maintenance, repair, and operation (MRO) products used to keep factories and construction sites running smoothly. The company's sales growth accelerated toward the end of 2025, but some investors are concerned that this was a temporary tailwind driven by a competitor's ransomware attack. This has caused many investors to scrutinize the month-to-month sales trends this year, which we believe has little bearing on the intrinsic value of the business. In our view, MonotaRO has the potential to retain much of the business picked up from this incident, as the company continues to demonstrate sturdy growth.
  • > FinecoBank is a savings platform in Italy offering a broad set of banking, brokerage, and wealth management services. While the business continues to generate positive results, the stock underperformed along with many other global financial services peers in response to concerns about AI software raising competitive pressure on human-led advisory services.

data.title: Enterprise Products: Macro Factors Keeping Things Interesting
data.description:
EPD First Quarter Review:

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) reported a generally excellent first quarter this morning. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $2.69 billion, up 10% versus last year and above Bloomberg consensus estimates of $2.62. Operational cash flow was $2.3 billion, up 10% from last year. Both it and adjusted EBITDA benefitted from last year's investments coming online, fewer investments this year, and volume increases, which included:

  • Record nat gas processing inlet volume up 7% and Permian inlet volumes up 9%.
  • Record pipeline transportation volumes of 14.2 MMBPD, up 7%
  • Record marine terminal volumes of 2.3 MMBPD, up 7%
  • Record NGL fractionation volumes of 1.9 MMBPD, up 16%

As expected, growth capital expenditure net of $600 million of asset sales ticked higher. The new number is $2.3-2.6 billion instead of $1.9-2.3 billion. Maintenance capex will be $580 million. I'd expect the growth capex number to go even higher, as almost $800 million was spent in Q1 already.

The good news is these higher capex numbers are still below last year. With higher EBITDA and cash flow, that makes the $.55/unit distributions and $116 million of buybacks only 57% of adjusted cash flow from operations.

The Iran War has changed the face of the energy industry. Most obviously, with the Straits of Hormuz closed to much traffic since the war started and closed completely by the US blockade since April 13th, ships are headed to the US Gulf Coast for the energy needs. Higher prices and higher volumes are a bonanza for US producers and owners of energy infrastructure. As you can see below, even with only a month of the conflict in the quarter, differentials were very strong for Q1.

Also, given this is EPD, which is traditionally natural gas and NGL driven, most of the growth this quarter came from those two segments.

It's funny. If you look back on the EPD Q4 presentation from February, this was the company's outlook for 2026.

Three things stick out for me from this projection. Two of them were from the "Under OPEC+" section. Namely, the idea that 3.5mm barrels of production quotas will be restored faster than expected will clearly not be the case. If anything, the war drove it in the opposite direction.

As I wrote yesterday, Iran's capacity, 3.5-4 million barrels, is going into storage on the mainland and on Kharg Island. If the Hormuz blockade doesn't end in the next few days, Iran will start shutting in some wells. It is estimated that a complete shut-in of Iran's wells could mean 300-500k barrels of daily production never coming back. That gives more weight to the last bullet point of the OPEC+ section, "Future status of sanctioned Russia, Iran, and Venezuela barrels remains a wildcard in global oil markets"

It also gives more weight to the second bullet of the Global Demand Growth section in the graphic above. "Low-cost U.S. feedstock advantage gives U.S. petrochemicals a significant profitability edge over all of Asia and Europe; even with transport costs, U.S. ethane remains the preferred ethylene feedstock globally." The disruption of energy markets has turbocharged this dynamic. Several international petrochemical producers have had to declare force majeure due to the inability to find enough feedstock. This is a massive tailwind to US petrochem producers, and even if Hormuz opened tomorrow, the shortage effects will not go away overnight.

Valuation:

As I mentioned in my comparison to ET, EPD does not offer EBITDA guidance but said in the Q4 call that adjusted EBITDA will be up modestly over 2025's $9.96 billion, which I took to mean low to mid-single-digits. Given Q1's 10% increase, I think it's very possible, if not likely, that growth for the year will ring in closer to high-single-digits. Annualizing Q1 puts that 2026 adjusted EBITDA around $10.8 billion. That brings EPD's enterprise value (@$38.22/unit) down to just below 11x EBITDA from the 11.2x EBITDA I used in the comparison to Energy Transfer (ET). It's still a premium ET, but the higher growth rate justifies the higher valuation a little more on an absolute basis.

Risk:

The risk, as always, is more in the daily swings in the unit prices than in the operations. As you can see in the graphic above, 80% of the EPD's operating margin is fee-based, and differentials rarely go below 15% of that operating margin. Given the Iran conflict dynamics and its impacts on commodity supply outside the US, I suspect volumes and differentials will be strong through at least the first half of the year. That said, if the conflict ended tomorrow and oil prices dropped suddenly, EPD unit prices would likely go down temporarily.

Conclusion:

I love EPD's assets, management team, and cash flows. Cash flows easily cover distributions, buybacks, and growth expenditure. Valuation is up, but growth is justifying it in my mind. This is a core holding for me and I like it for any income investor.

data.title: Heartland Value Strategy Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it. — George Santayana

Small caps outperformed large stocks by a wide margin at the start of the year, as AI-skepticism crept in and the outlook for the broader market improved. Then the war in Iran began and redirected investors’ attention in March back to bigger companies that were perceived as safe havens, raising the question: Which of these trends is the new normal?

While no one can say with absolute certainty, we’re reminded of the famous words of the philosopher and writer George Santayana, who warned of the perils of ignoring the lessons of history. The most important takeaway here is that all markets inevitably change. Every boom that has exalted a very narrow group of companies—from the ‘Nifty 50’ era in the 1970s to the dotcom bubble in the late ‘90s to the recent mega-cap tech rally driven by AI—has eventually come to an end. Over time, one way or another, the equity market has broadened out.

That began to take place over the past several months and we don’t believe rising geopolitical risks have derailed the long-term rotation into small and value. This shift is just getting started, based on a host of factors including improving fundamentals and historically attractive valuations. While the Russell 3000® Index trades at 17.5 times next year’s estimated earnings, our portfolio’s multiple is just 11.3X. Here’s another way to think about it: The value of the Russell 2000® Value Index is $1.5 trillion. That’s a mere 62% of Alphabet’s market capitalization and 50% of Nvidia’s. Small stocks aren’t just undervalued, they’re under-owned by institutions, individuals, and even foreign investors.

If the flight to safety persists in the short run, value stocks will likely come under pressure. But history tells us eventually, this part of the market should bounce back and outperform as risk aversion dissipates. Consider the chart below, which tracks the historic performance of the Russell 2000® Value Index relative to the Russell 2000® Growth benchmark against commodity prices. As you can see, past oil shocks have hurt small value initially. But over the long run, rising commodity prices have traditionally benefited this group as opportunities were uncovered and risk appetite normalized.

Source: FactSet Research Systems Inc. Monthly data 12/31/1988 to 2/28/2026. The data in this chart shows the relationship between commodity cycles and the relative performance of the Russell 2000® Value Index verses the Russell 2000 Growth Index stocks over time. Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000® companies with lower price/book ratios and lower forecasted growth characteristics. Russell 2000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000® companies with higher price/book ratios and higher forecasted growth characteristics. The S&P GSCI (formerly Goldman Sachs Commodity Index) is a premier, production-weighted benchmark for the global commodity market, measuring performance through liquid futures contracts. It provides investors with broad, diversified exposure, covering energy, industrial metals, agriculture, livestock, and precious metals, with significant weighting often placed on energy products. All indices are unmanaged. It is not possible to invest in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

This gives us confidence about the long-term prospects ahead for small value. So, too, does the fact that our holdings are increasingly being viewed as takeout targets by the private markets and other publicly traded companies. We’re heartened to see other investors are finally appreciating what we’ve known all along—the attractive valuations for the businesses in our portfolio.

During the first quarter, for example, Calavo Growers (CVGW), a marketer and distributor of avocados and guacamole, agreed to be acquired by its biggest publicly owned rival. We began to accumulate shares of this company in early 2025, which we have previously highlighted as an example of the types of stocks our process identifies. That was shortly after its long-time former CEO returned to stabilize operations. Prior to that, the company endured a prolonged period of mismanagement under several chief executives.

At Heartland, our security selection system is guided by our 10 Principles of Value Investing™, and one of those precepts directs us to companies with capable management and insider ownership. Calavo showed how important effective management can be for creating shareholder value, as evidenced by our brief but fruitful investment in the stock.

Another holding that was taken out in Q1 was SunOpta (STKL). Late last year, we added to our exposure to this leading plant-based milk producer after a few key customers accelerated their supply decisions, forcing management to reset earnings expectations, which sent the shares plummeting. We thought the market overreacted and were encouraged by strong insider buying. This patience paid off, and the stock was one of the contributors to our performance at the start of the year.

These are just two examples of how our 10 Principles of Value Investing™ have served us and our clients well for more than four decades. We’re confident that its focus on fundamental factors such as low valuations, high-quality balance sheets, and positive earnings dynamics will continue to set us up for success in the current market and beyond.

Attribution Analysis & Portfolio Activity

In the first quarter, the Heartland Value Fund gained 7.46%, compared with the 4.96% return for the Russell 2000® Value Index. The Strategy outperformed its benchmark over the past three months, as it has over the past one, three, five, and 10 years.

Stock picking accounted for most of our outperformance, as has been the case over the past one, three, and five years. Our selection effect was negative in only three sectors during the quarter: Energy, Industrials, and Utilities. Moreover, it turned positive in Health Care, which had been a challenging group for us late last year, thanks in part to a handful of new positions in the sector.

During the quarter, we continued to remix within sectors, taking gains in winners and reallocating to new ideas where we see attractive risk/reward factors. For example, two of our real estate investment trust holdings, were acquired by private buyers. This allowed us to redistribute funds from names where much of the gains had already been realized into other REITs with strong potential catalysts for recognition and identifiable upside.

When identifying companies that fit our process, it is important that management demonstrate the ability to course correct, even if they have hit certain hurdles in the past. Case in point: Allegiant (ALGT). We initiated a position in the ultra low cost airline last year, when the shares were depressed and its story was obscured by a non core hotel project that consumed capital and complicated the narrative.

The airline ultimately sold the hotel asset, took the financial hit, and refocused on the core business—a prime example of management taking its lumps and working to improve capital allocation. The stock subsequently rallied significantly from the lows, driven by this refocus, improved execution, and the carrier’s low cost model.

Allegiant is facing renewed pressure as oil and jet fuel prices climbed after the start of the war in Iran, reminding investors of the sector’s sensitivity to energy. Recently, we reallocated capital from other airline exposure into ALGT to ensure we held the name that had the most compelling risk/reward outlook, even if its story is masked by near-term macro headwinds centered on fuel costs.

This carrier enjoys a real niche, as it is focused on leisure travel and 75% of its routes do not have non-stop competition. This model has held up well during past slowdowns and fuel spikes, and we expect it will again. Yet the stock closed the quarter at $83 a share. Longer-term, we believe that the ALGT could trade at 1.75x EV/Sales, which would value the shares at more than $200.

There has been a lot of carnage in the software industry lately, on fears that AI bots will take over the business. i3 Verticals (IIIV) hasn’t been immune.

The company, which makes enterprise software for the public sector, including school payment systems, has been pressured by the broader narrative that AI will upend its software business models. Its shares have slumped to the low thirties last fall and closed the quarter at $22. In our view, this is a case where the market has over generalized the risk. For starters, i3 provides embedded software and payments solutions for niche verticals, such as school cafeteria and tuition payments systems. The retention rate for these types of contracts has historically been very high and switching vendors can be complex. Municipalities and school districts are not in the habit of frequently putting out Requests for Proposals to change systems.

Yet the company’s cash flow characteristics, sticky customer base, and potential for eventual strategic interest do not seem to be fully reflected in current valuations. We believe IIIV is worth 16x EBITDA, which would return the stock to around $32 a share. Even if it were to hit that multiple, though, it would still be trading at a steep discount to its largest peer, which trades at 19x EBITDA.

Chiron Real Estate (XRN) is another example of how important management can be to our decision-making process. This real estate investment trust manages a portfolio of medical-related real estate primarily consisting of outpatient medical and inpatient rehab.

XRN was known as Global Medical REIT when we initiated the position last year, at around the time the company named Mark Decker, Jr., as CEO. He had previously turned around another REIT we followed and moved swiftly to cut out non-core assets, expand the portfolio into better performing subsectors, and align himself with shareholders. After outlining his vision and strategy for the company, Decker personally initiated an open market purchase of over $1 million and swiftly established a share repurchase program.

The management change and insider buying was a strong positive in our view. The executive officers, directors, and employees who now collectively control 8.3% of XRN’s equity, understand how undervalued XRN is at 12x EV/EBITDA, well below the 14-17x range for most of its Healthcare REIT peers.

We believe the recent CEO buys also validate our view that the stock is priced well below the intrinsic value of Chiron’s Real Estate. The stock currently trades close to a 30% discount to analysts’ consensus estimate of NAV, and we think that gap will close as management continues to execute on its vision and strategy.

Outlook

Recent volatility, sparked by geopolitical developments, may have momentarily hit pause on the rotation from mega cap tech to small value. But, in our opinion, it hasn’t done away with the fundamental case for this part of the market. If anything, we believe opportunities have only increased, and it’s our job as disciplined active managers to identify them. With over 40 years of experience, we know that times like this are precisely when to be opportunistic, not discouraged. Over the past several months, we have been using the strong performance in small value to remix and upgrade our holdings with overlooked stocks that have yet to be re-rated by investors. We will continue to do so during this recent bout of volatility, while being guided by our 10 Principles that have served us well for over four decades.

Thank you for your loyalty and continued confidence. Fundamentally yours,

The Heartland Investment Team

data.title: Ares Capital: No Evidence Of SaaS Pain
data.description:
Troubles in the private credit market, which are related to concerns about the performance of SaaS loans especially, have taken a heavy toll on the BDC sector so far in 2026, including Ares Capital (ARCC). Ares Capital is a premier BDC given its decade-long history of strong private credit performance, but the investment firm has seen a contraction of its valuation multiplier this year as investors rotated out of private credit, fearing potentially disruptive impacts of the deployment of AI.

Yet, this rotation of capital out of the private credit sector is what could present investors with an opportunity to earn above-average returns in 2026 and beyond, especially since the BDC maintained stable credit quality in the first quarter and managed to fully support its dividend with net investment income. At the moment, shares of Ares Capital trade below net asset value and below the P/NAV average of the last three years.

Previous Rating

I rated Ares Capital a 'Hold' in February -- A 10% Yield For Trying Times -- due to the investment firm having historically low loan loss rates but suffering pressure on its dividend coverage profile amid a faster pace of loan repayments. While the BDC managed to fully support its dividend with net investment income in the first quarter, private credit concerns continue to impact the sector, creating valuation pressure even for the best BDCs in the market. Since Ares Capital grew its portfolio by ~9% year-over-year and has good dividend coverage, I believe the current setup continues to warrant a 'Hold' rating for the private credit investment platform.

Strong Value Proposition as Investors Fear AI-Driven SaaS Headwinds

Ares Capital published a total portfolio value of $29.5B in Q1'26, showing a 9% increase compared to the year-earlier period, mainly due to new originations in the first lien category. A total of 73% of new gross funding commitments fell into the first lien category in the first quarter, highlighting the importance of this specific asset group for the company's net investment income growth (discussed further below). The majority of the investment firm's portfolio consisted of first liens as of the end of Q1'26, as the investment company managed to slightly increase its first lien percentage from 58% in Q1'25 to 60% in Q1'26.

As far as the BDC's non-accrual percentages go, Ares Capital continued to excel in the first fiscal quarter and published a non-accrual ratio of 1.2%, based off of fair value, unchanged from the previous quarter. This stable credit profile highlights that so far AI has not emerged as a major risk factor in Ares Capital's portfolio. For comparison, Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which have not yet posted their first-quarter results, had non-accrual percentages of 0.5% and 1.1% at fair value in Q4'25.

The table below shows the progression of Ares Capital's non-accrual percentage, based off of both cost and fair value, in the last year.

Portfolio, Non-Accrual Trend Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 Y/Y Growth
Portfolio Value, fair value $27,130 $27,886 $28,693 $29,485 $29,499 8.7%
Non-Accrual Amount (cost, $M) $414 $546 $509 $524 $614 48.3%
In Percent 1.5% 2.0% 1.8% 1.8% 2.1% 40.0%
Non-Accrual Amount (fair value, $M) $244 $327 $289 $350 $345 41.4%
In Percent 0.9% 1.2% 1.0% 1.2% 1.2% 33.3%

Source: Author

Ares Capital's portfolio growth, driven by new first lien originations, results in a nice uptick in the firm's interest income. The main source of the BDC's income, interest, grew 5% year-over-year to $550M, with both a larger portfolio as well as a delay in interest rate cuts benefiting Ares Capital.

Ares Capital's dividend coverage ratio sequentially improved, indicating that investors may indeed be too pessimistic about the BDC's NII potential. In the most recent quarter, BDC supported its dividend obligation of $0.48 per share quarterly with net investment income easily: it generated a dividend coverage ratio of ~115%. It was the second straight quarter of improving dividend coverage for Ares Capital, which translates into a higher safety margin for investors than just 3-6 months ago.

ARCC Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 Y/Y Growth
NII $0.54 $0.49 $0.48 $0.52 $0.55 1.9%
Dividend $0.48 $0.48 $0.48 $0.48 $0.48 0.0%
Coverage Trend 112.5% 102.1% 100.0% 108.3% 114.6% 1.9%

Source: Author

Private Credit Outlook

The impact of artificial intelligence has heavily weighed on the private credit market lately and led to a major contraction of NAV multipliers in the BDC sector. According to Moody's Private Credit 2026 Outlook, however, the sector will continue to be an attractive market for capital deployment, simply because companies will need more money to grow their operations. The main reason for this is that higher-risk business lending is still shunned by traditional banks -- a consequence of the financial crisis in 2007 -- which has led to private credit platforms like Ares Capital taking market share. Moody's projects that the size of the private credit market will grow close to $4 trillion by the end of the decade, up from about $2.5 trillion this year.

Further, Ares Capital has 22% exposure to the software industry, which is not especially high in the market. This exposure compares to comparable software/SaaS percentages for Blue Owl Capital and Blackstone Secured Lending of 11% and 21%. For comparison, Hercules Capital (HTGC), a leading BDC focused on loan origination in the software sector, had a 35% SaaS percentage in the fourth quarter. Since Ares Capital's Q1'26 scorecard did not indicate any change in credit/loan performance, I believe investors may exaggerate the sector's credit woes.

Ares Capital's Valuation

Ares Capital is certainly not alone in trading at a discount to net asset value right now: shares of the BDC are currently trading at a price-to-NAV ratio of 0.93X, which compares against P/NAV ratios of 0.88X for Blackstone Secured Lending and 0.73X for Blue Owl Capital.

Ares Capital's net asset value as of the end of Q1'26 was $19.59 per share, showing a 1.8% Q/Q decline rate, mainly because of net unrealized (losses) on investments of $357M (mainly unrealized depreciation). I believe that private credit sector fears are widely exaggerated, and Ares Capital's low non-accrual percentage and solid NII performance highlight that the BDC has revaluation potential.

In order to remain on the safe side in case I am wrong about the impact of AI on SaaS loans longer term, however, and in order to reflect the risk of rate cuts in 2026 if inflation cools (ARCC has about a 71% variable rate asset base), I am maintaining my 'Hold' rating for Ares Capital for now.

Risks With Ares Capital

If I am wrong about the risk that AI poses to software companies and their business models, Ares Capital could see an increase in loan default rates in the software/SaaS category, which may negatively impact the BDC's NII performance. So far, based off of the company's Q1 report, this risk has not yet become a reality. A drop in the dividend coverage ratio below 100% or a rise in the non-accrual percentage above 2.0% may be reasons for me to downgrade ARCC, as the BDC could then face deteriorating dividend safety.

Final Thoughts

Ares Capital may suffer from negative investment sentiment surrounding the private credit sector, but the investment firm's actual financial performance for Q1'26 was rock solid: the BDC is growing its portfolio and net investment income and published stable balance sheet quality quarter-over-quarter.

Most importantly, Ares Capital has a long history of prudent loan underwriting, and the BDC is supporting its dividend with net investment income for two consecutive quarters, with growing safety margins. Ares Capital has historically traded at a premium to net asset value, and the risk of a dividend reset in the short term is low, in my opinion.

However, given general sentiment risk as it relates to private credit and AI disruption, as well as the risk of lower base rates in 2026, I do not feel compelled to change my rating for Ares Capital just yet.

data.title: Annaly Capital: Double-Digit Yield + Good Appreciation Potential
data.description:
My Investing Philosophy

I like dividends. I also like to find companies that are out of favor and selling at a discount to their respective fair values.

I use multiple sources of analysis and expected growth rates as well as articles, opinions, and corporate data releases/presentations. But I prefer to discern from all that for myself what I really expect from a company and then plug my assumptions into my models to find what I believe to be the true fair value of a company based upon discounted future cash flows and my own required rate of return. My models do not work for valuing this company, so I will use a different approach.

I like to add stocks of companies to my portfolio that I expect to have competitive advantages, good management, and strong balance sheets combined with a business model that has weathered multiple periods of economic stress and geopolitical turmoil only to emerge stronger, demonstrating resilience. Check out some of my other articles here.

Company Background

Annaly Capital Management (NLY) invests primarily in government agency-backed securities with mostly residential property as the underlying assets. It also invests (to a lesser degree) in non-agency-backed commercial mortgage loans for real estate. It is organized as a real estate investment trust, or R.E.I.T., so that it is not subject to federal income tax to the extent that it distributes at least 90% of earnings to its shareholders.

The company has navigated all sorts of economic environments for over three decades and usually sports a double-digit dividend yield. It rallied during and after the financial crisis of 2007-09 when real estate values dropped primarily because its holdings were guaranteed by the federal government. Management’s strategy has been put to the test and passed each time over the years.

It is often used as a flight to safety, as explained in a good article by Rida Morwa recently. When the economy is in trouble, the Fed tends to cut interest rates, which lowers the cost of capital for NLY, improving its margins and profitability. As you can see by the above chart, NLY is near its all-time low. Full disclosure: I bought a bunch when it was trading below $19 and yielding 14%. I plan to hold most of my position until it hits at least $40/share. It has proven a great place to hold excess cash while I look for other bargains.

Earnings

Q1 results included EAD (Earnings Available for Distribution) EPS (Earnings Per Share) were $0.76/sh., beating consensus of $0.74 and rising from $0.72 in Q1 of last year.

Q1 net interest income of $452.7 million from $366.6 million in Q4 and $220.0 million in Q1 of 2024. Net interest spread also increased from the year-ago period to $1.42% from 1.35%.

Q1 financial highlights are shown below:

This is not the normal company that can generate steady, predictable earnings growth. Instead, its earnings generally fluctuate (for the most part) inversely to interest rates. Management uses hedging strategies to offset adverse conditions and potential short-term volatility with good success.

NLY was reporting losses for several years as interest rates rose because it holds much of its assets as “available for sale.” That is an accounting term that requires those securities to be “marked to market” at the end of each reporting period. Such securities must be listed at market value and will require write-downs when the respective value falls, which is what happens to fixed-rate mortgage securities when interest rates rise. So, NLY was forced to report paper (non-cash) losses. The inverse is true when interest rates fall; those same assets increase in market value, resulting in paper gains and higher profits.

To explain the falling price prior to 2022, when interest rates rose, we need to take a look at the economic environment in two separate stages: 2012-2020 and 2020-2022. Below is how I read the cause and effect with NLY management’s reactions to the changing environments. I added the long-term chart again for easy reference.

From 2012 to 2020, the Fed was using QE (Quantitative Easing, or printing money) to increase liquidity in the economy (more specifically, to prop up the financial sector) after the housing/financial crisis. The Fed also lowered interest rates to near zero, creating both opportunities and displacements within different sectors of the economy. There was much uncertainty in financial markets during this period, causing NLY management to become more cautious, using less leverage and generating less revenue. Also, the interest rate environment changed unfavorably for mortgage REITs, as the spreads that could be earned from low-interest-bearing mortgages over borrowing costs narrowed dramatically.

Just as management began to get acclimated to the new interest rate environment and began to increase its leverage and holdings, the COVID-19 pandemic hit. Management of NLY, like that of many companies, pulled back dramatically due to the economic uncertainty, causing a contraction in earnings resulting in a dip in dividends paid. Investors reacted negatively to the lowered dividends, and the price fell some more.

Then, in 2022, the Fed raised interest rates, causing NLY assets to fall in market value, resulting in a reported loss in earnings for 2023 as the full brunt of the asset valuation was felt (paper losses). Dividends remained relatively flat from 2022 through 2024, as the share price continued to trade within a range between $17.50 and $20.

But as interest rose, taking mortgage rates higher, NLY management began to change its outlook and expand its asset base more aggressively. Then, in 2025, as interest rates began to fall again, NLY assets increased in value while interest rate spreads expanded, resulting in both paper gains and cash profits generation improvement. NLY raised its dividend, and the stock price began what I believe will be an extended period of improving fundamentals for the company. If you want to understand my expectations for the economy of the coming five years, please consider my recent article, “ The Global Economy in Five Years.”

Growth Prospects

I expect interest rates to fall once the Iran conflict ends and once the Fed chair candidate, Kevin Warsh, is confirmed and takes the reins. I do not expect a rapid drop in rates, but more of a managed lowering over multiple quarters, if not years. It should not contain many surprises and should be relatively predictable, in my opinion. That should make it much easier for NLY to add assets and improve its margins with increased leverage. Predictability is the key.

Below is a slide from NLY’s Q1 Earnings Presentation that explains management’s view of the macro environment.

Like me, NLY management believes in the resilience of the U.S. economy with temporarily rising inflation that should fall in the 2nd half of 2026. The technical assessment for fixed income fund flows remains strong and will likely continue.

Mortgage rates rose, and NLY management added assets with increased leverage. Now, we wait for the borrowing costs to fall to increase margins further over the next few years. Being an REIT, NLY will be required by law to increase its dividends as earnings increase, and I expect the stock price to follow the same trend as dividends.

Profitability

Profits should rise as borrowing costs fall with interest rates over the next few years. If you refer back to the long-term chart earlier in this article, you’ll see how much that can affect the price. But the price merely follows the trend set by the dividends, which, again, rise with earnings. Earnings will rise as the margins improve. It’s just math.

Balance Sheet

As you can see from the table below, assets have been increased aggressively to take advantage of an environment of interest rate spreads likely to widen over time.

The graphic below illustrates NLY’s investment strategy, providing a breakdown of allocations across its three main holdings.

Dividend

This being an REIT, I am comfortable with the payout ratio of nearly 95% and with a yield of 12.37%, as well as my expectation for an improving net interest margin; the lack of a consistently rising dividend doesn’t bother me either.

I fully expect the dividend to rise in 2027 through 2030, barring some unforeseen geopolitical event that would dramatically change the long-term prospects and trends underlying inflation and interest rates.

As we see below, NLY has consistently paid a dividend for 28 consecutive years, or since its inception.

The dividend has ebbed and flowed inversely with interest rates, for the most part. We may not be heading as high as the 2003 or 2010 peaks, but I think we should see the dividend climb back well over $1/share between now and 2030. A 60% rise in the dividend should also support a stock price rise of a similar magnitude. And the next peak could be closer to double where the dividend stands today.

Valuation

NLY does not fit my DDM (Dividend Discount Model) the way most dividend-paying stocks do. So, I decided it would be more relevant to consider NLY by looking at it from a historical perspective. The long-term average PE is 8. The current PE is 7.6, so the stock is trading at a slight discount from its historical multiple valuation. But I believe that the interest rate environment (increasing spreads) will improve over the next five years to levels similar to those existing prior to the last series of rate hikes by the Fed. Interest rates do not need to fall to the same level as in 2021 to reach the same levels of spread. We just need the spread between borrowing costs and mortgage rates to expand to cause the improvement. Thus, I expect to see NLY valued more in line with pre-Covid times, or closer to $40/share.

Current assets (mortgages and loans) are yielding close to 6% on average and are long-term (not maturing for two decades or more). As short-term interest rates fall, NLY will be able to refinance more of its debt with short-term instruments, which could dramatically lower its overall borrowing cost, resulting in a wider interest rate spread and higher net interest rate spread (rising profitability).

For this reason, I believe that the price will hit $40 by 2030, creating a compound total annual return potential of 7.3%, not including dividends (which would increase it to over 20%). The Excel formula is =(($40/$22.75)^(1/5))-1

Risks

A global recession is not out of the question if the Iranian regime finds a way to hold out for several months. If that happens, there won’t be many places to invest. I explain why I don’t expect that scenario to play out in the article linked earlier.

The Fed could keep interest rates higher for much longer than expected, in which case I would just have to hold onto my shares, collecting the 12.3% dividend until the stalemate ends. That is not such a terrible fate for a retiree like myself. But, with AI, robotics, and automation all likely to strain the employment picture over the coming few years, I am inclined to believe that the Fed will be forced to lower rates to support a deteriorating employment environment.

Conclusion

As a retiree, I am happy to collect a 12.3% dividend for as long as it takes for the appreciation to materialize. It has been a rewarding wait so far as I have enjoyed a stock price rise of nearly 20% while also collecting my juicy, double-digit dividends.

I think NLY is a BUY for investors looking for current income and a potential 75% capital gain by 2030.

data.title: Coupang's Infrastructure Advantage Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Coupang's (CPNG) investment case is based upon the significant logistical structure that it has developed, making it a critical part of the national delivery infrastructure rather than just a traditional e-commerce platform. The company has built over 100 logistics/delivery centers throughout South Korea, with 70% of citizens living within 7 miles of a Coupang distribution center. With this large-scale logistical structure, the company has established an infrastructure moat around their logistics operations. The combination of Coupang's logistics infrastructure and fast delivery times creates an opportunity to expand margins as automation technology becomes increasingly adopted in its operations. Given that automation technology currently accounts for ~10% of Coupang's total infrastructure, there are opportunities for cost savings that could help improve sentiment without continued revenue growth.

Coupang has begun a transition from investing heavily in new businesses and technologies to focusing on cash flow. This is particularly apparent in the Product Commerce division, which produces solid margins of 8.4% and strong EBITDA growth. However, consolidated profitability has remained depressed as a result of investments made in Taiwan, Eats, and the integration of Farfetch. These three areas represent potential future sources of earnings in the long-term story, with early indicators suggesting that both the Taiwan and Farfetch divisions could grow into meaningful sources of profitability. The company has also established a WOW membership program, designed to retain customers through loyalty programs and increase customer spending. This contributes to higher spend and increased monetization, creating a long-term flywheel that increases customer value. With short-term noise overshadowing the company's long-term potential, I'm rating the stock a Strong Buy.

A Logistics Fortress

Coupang's primary competitive advantage is its complex logistical network. The company now operates over 100 fulfillment centers throughout South Korea, owns its own private fleet for last-mile delivery, and has invested billions to build out this physical network. The result is that nearly 70% of South Korea is within 7 miles of a Coupang distribution center, allowing next-day delivery.

The difficulty in replicating this network is both due to the level of investment required as well as the years of operational experience already embedded at the company. In 2024 alone, Coupang was able to increase same-day and dawn deliveries by 45% while reducing line haul costs by 16% using a combination of robotic technology and process improvements. Despite this improvement, the automated share of total infrastructure is currently in the very low teens. Management has continually highlighted the potential for additional margin gains through continued use of automated processes, which could set them further apart from competitors. With the company's market share of ~40%, Coupang has opportunities to grow and become more efficient, making today's levels appear undervalued.

High-Burn to Cash Generation

Adjusted EBITDA for Product Commerce rose to $2.485 billion in FY 2025, up from $2.0 billion in FY 2024. Segment margins improved to 8.4% YoY, showing signs of operational leverage. Growth rates for revenue increased 16% in constant currency, and gross profit grew 22% in constant currency, reflecting the increasing impact from the FLC third-party marketplace offering, where revenues are reported on a net basis. This compresses revenue growth while gross profit continues to outpace it, signaling that the business mix may be improving.

Overall, revenues for the company totaled $34.5 billion for FY 2025, up 14% YoY and 18% in constant currency, with gross margin increasing to 29.4%. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for Coupang was $1.49 billion at a margin of 4.3%; however, this number is impacted negatively by losses (~$995 million) from Taiwan development, Farfetch integration, and Eats expansion. This makes the Product Commerce margin of 8.4% a better indicator of earnings power.

FCF was $527 million in FY 2025 versus $1 billion in FY 2024 due to the data incident, which increased working capital, as well as greater investments in automation. The company also has a solid balance sheet with ~$6.3 billion in cash vs. ~$4.6 billion in debt, resulting in a net cash position of $1.68 billion. Due to this, management has been returning capital by purchasing back $243 million worth of its stock in 2025. This signals a shift away from heavy reinvestment and towards increasing shareholder returns, with a total authorization of $1 billion for buybacks. Coupang's improved balance sheet (with $6 billion+ in cash) also supports this and allows for flexibility to either invest in growth or continue to return value back to shareholders. Although there has been some hiccup in results, the long-term trajectory remains the same, giving investors the chance to buy a maturing high-growth business at a discount.

Beyond Korea

Coupang’s launch of Rocket Delivery into Taiwan during late 2022 has shown strong results. Despite heavy near-term investment in this region, management has highlighted triple-digit revenue growth in Taiwan, suggesting that the long-term payoff will be worth it as the market matures. By the end of 2024, revenue in Taiwan grew by 23% QoQ, and this momentum carried into 2025. Besides Rocket Delivery, Coupang has introduced their WOW membership program into Taiwan, showing signs of sufficient market density and consumer trust.

When Coupang purchased Farfetch in early 2024, Farfetch was losing hundreds of millions annually with declining growth metrics. One year after acquiring the company, Farfetch improved from heavy losses to a breakeven run rate while maintaining 49 million monthly site visitors and ~$4 billion in annual transaction volume globally. Management’s outlook involves further streamlining operations and removing what it described as “non-reflective” cost structures, which should help improve gross margins as integration matures.

With this acquisition, the company has positioned itself as a legitimate player in the global luxury goods market, opening up more growth potential beyond its flagship e-commerce business.

Net revenue for Developing Offerings was $4.94 billion during FY 2025, a 38% increase YoY and a 40% YoY increase in constant currency. The losses of $995 million were due to investments in infrastructure in Taiwan and costs associated with integrating Farfetch and were not structural issues with the segment. These developments give Coupang optionality and could contribute meaningfully as they continue to scale and improve, which is potential that doesn't currently appear to be factored into the valuation.

The WOW Flywheel

Coupang’s WOW membership program is probably one of the most successful retention programs in Asian e-commerce. During mid-2024, the company raised the monthly fee by 58%, which many assumed would lead to massive churn. It did not. Before that, in 2021, the company increased the price of the membership by 72%, which also had very few member losses. The reason members continue to stay is because active WOW members who use all five benefits will save ~970,000 won per year (~$655), which is much more than the annual membership cost of ~95,000 won. This makes the value proposition attractive and allows them to maintain members despite price increases.

WOW membership experienced cancellations due to the data incident in December 2025, something management admitted on the Q4 2025 earnings call. However, a recovery trend in WOW memberships began to appear once it became clear no sensitive information was compromised. With this recovery and the stock still trading below prior levels, it creates a buying opportunity. What makes WOW so important to Coupang is the effects it has on the rest of the business. Members shop more frequently, spend more per visit, and have much higher lifetime values. This creates an amplifier effect on advertising and fintech products from Coupang, where a denser active member base creates greater demand from third-party sellers/advertisers. This creates a compounding loop that makes the Korean business hard to replace.

Upcoming Earnings

There are three key factors to watch as Coupang reports its Q1 earnings. These include the company's ability to leverage automation to increase margins, narrow losses in “Developing Offerings,” and continue WOW member recovery. Product Commerce's adjusted EBITDA margin is a key metric, and the continued growth of this metric (from 8.4% in FY 2025) will signal improvement in automation and strengthen its logistics moat. Management still has confidence in the long-term future for the Product Commerce segment and believes that it will be able to achieve 10%+ EBITDA margins. This expansion is expected to be driven by operational efficiencies, supply chain optimization, and operating leverage, alongside a shift toward higher-margin product mix. Another aspect to watch is improvement in the profitability of the Taiwan and Farfetch businesses, where the market may be expecting improvement as operations mature.

Finally, it will be important to hear commentary on WOW membership trends post-data incident. Management has already noted that the impact from the data incident began to stabilize across growth rates, active customers, and WOW memberships in early Q1 2026, suggesting underlying demand remains intact. If Coupang can reaffirm member retention rates have returned to pre-data incident levels, it will provide further confirmation of the resiliency of Coupang’s flywheel model. This will suggest that the company has sufficient pricing power and market share to continue to operate profitably, despite competition from lower-cost players. Strong or weak metrics in these areas could have a huge impact on investor sentiment.

Valuation

At the current price of ~$21, Coupang is trading at around 1.04x EV/TTM sales and ~35x EV/TTM EBITDA. It should be noted that the EV/EBITDA number is somewhat depressed by the ~$995M in Developing Offerings investment losses. As such, using the Product Commerce segment as a base would place the company at ~14x EV/EBITDA (with $2.49 billion in segment-adjusted EBITDA). This represents a relatively low multiple for the company's strong competitive advantage combined with 88 bps of annual margin expansion and expanding automation efficiency.

For FY2026, I expect adjusted EBITDA to reach ~$2.8 Billion due to the resumption of growth through mid-single to high single-digit rates on a constant-currency basis as well as continuing margin expansion. I also project that Developing Offerings will reduce their net loss to the ~$600-$700 Million range as Farfetch reaches profitability and Taiwan grows. This brings total adjusted EBITDA for the company to ~$2.1-2.2 billion for FY2026. Applying a 22x EV/EBITDA, which is similar to peers such as Sea Ltd.'s (SE) and Amazon (AMZN), brings EV to $48 billion. Adding $1.68 billion in net cash and dividing by 1.83 billion shares outstanding gives a stock price of ~$27. I believe this is a fair 12-month price target given Taiwan optionality and the potential for Farfetch to recover.

Risks to the Thesis

The major risk to Coupang’s stock is regulation, with investigations still ongoing. This makes potential fines or regulatory actions uncertain. Forensic analysis was completed by Mandiant and Palo Alto Networks, which included both digital forensics and threat hunting activities, and neither could find evidence of customers' personal financial information being compromised. Coupang also compensated the 33.7 million customers affected with vouchers worth 50,000 won, which could help keep users on the platform. This suggests the incident is a near-term headwind rather than a long-term drag.

AliExpress and Temu are gaining market share in Korean e-commerce markets through low pricing, creating competition for Coupang. However, the fact that the company has continued to raise its WOW membership fee demonstrates that the price alone does not necessarily drive away existing customers. Coupang also offers a number of additional services, including dawn delivery, fresh produce, free returns, OTT streaming, and integrated financial services, which cannot currently be replicated by any cross-border marketplace.

Takeaway

Coupang represents a strong combination of an infrastructure moat, improving operating margins, and undervalued international operations. The market appears to have discounted both the ability of the Korean segment to generate profits in the short term, as well as the potential profitability of newer segments in the long term. This creates a disconnect from the stock's fair value, which was caused by short-term headwinds.

As the business continues to automate at scale, normalize investments, and grow new geographic markets, it has the opportunity to expand profitability. With a big change in the stock's valuation but little change in the long-term story, I'm rating the stock a Strong Buy with a 12-month price target of ~$27.

data.title: Western Digital: This Bull Run May Just Be Starting
data.description:
Western Digital Corporation (WDC) creates and sells data storage devices and solutions based on hard disk drive technology. The firm is set to report FY 26 Q3 earnings with expectations of massive YoY expansion on earnings and sales. The stock is up nearly 9x in the last year alone, and investors that have not gotten in on the gains yet are left wondering if it's too late.

Their products are used in everything from laptops to large cloud data centers. Almost 90% of their revenue now comes from sales in the cloud space to specific cloud providers and enterprise data centers. A tight supply and rising demand environment have led to capped-out capacity for WDC, a good and bad thing. The industry is seeing explosive growth, and longer-term contracts are giving suppliers like WDC more pricing power than in the past. Valuation is raised, but not horribly. Because of these industry headwinds and their ability to create greater margins on more efficient exabyte shipments, I have WDC as a buy.

Q3 Expectations

Adjusted EPS is projected to arrive at $2.39 per share, which would be a YoY change of 75% greater. All 20 analysts on Seeking Alpha have given the stock upward revisions in the last 90 days. You can see the growth above in EPS estimates and how rapid it is expected, especially in the next 2-3 years.

Another piece that I am bullish about is where I think they sit in the hard disk drive cycle. Amidst the growth the stock has seen by price return, earnings, and demand, revenue has actually been lower YoY for 4 straight quarters. This week they are anticipated to report sales of $3.23 billion, which would reverse the recent trend and put them 40% higher YoY. I think this can be a huge sign that even though the stock has seen huge growth, this could actually be in the early stages of a new cycle. Storage prices are cyclical, and WDC saw a big drop in revenue over the last few years. This could actually be the beginning of a new upcycle for them, exactly what investors should want to hear.

A few things to look out for in management commentary and reported data this week are what they are seeing on prices per exabyte, how many exabytes are being shipped, any new long-term commitments, and what their plan is to continue increasing efficiency at an already full capacity. They have outlined that they expect to be north of 50% in gross margin and 40% in operating margin, which was actually negative not too long ago in 2024. Also, I will be curious to see further comments on the AI demand from the large data center customers. WDC has seen their revenue from their top 10 customers grow from 65% to 77% in the first 6 months of the fiscal year over the last two years. This comes as these big-time AI infrastructure-needy customers continue to up their needs. This is good, but with a cyclical industry, you don't want too high of a dependence on just a few customers.

Growing Exabytes Are the Answer

When WDC “ships” more exabytes, it means that they sold more drives or higher capacity that can store more data, which leads to greater sales. In Q2 they increased their exabytes shipped by over 20% YoY and are now on 4 straight quarters of growth here. This is the real indicator of demand for their product, which is primarily driven by large hyperscalers, like AWS and Microsoft, for example. Their cloud segment has come to dominate their revenue totals as these cloud-storage providers look for hard disk drives for massive datasets. Long-term agreements are becoming the norm in this space, and WDC has publicized that they have established two long-term agreements for 2027 and already one for 2028. They note that they are set with purchase orders from each of their seven biggest customers.

A massive driver for WDC is the price per terabyte (TB) for hard drives. If you look at the firm's history of revenue, it's not hard to realize that price per TB peaked in the mid-2010s, as did WDC revenue. In a rough period from 2018 to 2022, tech improvements increased storage density, and cloud customers gained pricing power. These customers also overbought and laid off purchases through a lot of the last several years. Price per TB was north of $30 a decade ago, has dropped down near $13 in 2024, and now looks to be on the horizon of a rebound as demand rebounds strongly.

The fact that WDC still has relatively low revenue based on historical numbers and has been able to grow earnings like they have, I think, is a great sign. Especially as it seems they are on the cusp of reporting their first YoY revenue win in a while, marking a sign that the tides could be turning.

Efficient Growth

WDC has said that their capacity for 2026 is already fully booked. This is a fantastic sign for demand purposes but leaves investors wondering how they could grow from here. They, of course, can slowly build on more operations, but management has made it clear that is not the key to the growth plan.

They are rapidly advancing higher-capacity drives, which are typically around 20TB today. They have 40TB drives that are already in customer testing, with a roadmap to scale to 60TB and even 100TB over time, dramatically increasing total exabytes shipped per unit. They plan for deployment of the 40TB drives later this year and for 100TB drives by 2029. So, instead of needing to sell more units, WDC is well into the process of increasing exabytes shipped by improving their hard drive storage capacity.

Risk to Thesis & Peers

WDC has seen their PE ratio rise from 8x to 38x in the last year. That's obviously a risk and a large rise in valuation. Compared to the industry, they are actually only slightly above the sector median, which is giving me peace of mind from this perspective. Price to sales is 4x the sector median, but their PEG is astronomically low at 0.04x, which shows clear expectations of growth that I have already outlined their path toward. I think that the stock is slightly overvalued, but I am not concerned. The elevated multiples will compress quickly if their earnings rise as expected this year, by estimates of around 82%.

Competitor Seagate Tech (STX) is another key player in this field that has also seen a massive price return of 6.5x in the last year. They are also working toward increasing TB per drive and have already rolled out drives of 30TB+. I think there is more than enough demand in the cloud-driven world that WDC does not need to worry about losing out. But it is a competitive space, and there are others honing in on similar strategies. Comparing further, WDC has had slightly better growth in sales than STX and is valued at much more of a discount, as STX currently carries a PE of 67x. STX at the moment is more of the pure-play hard disk drive investment, whereas WDC has a more diversified structure. However, the spinoff of Sandisk (SNDK) from WDC has led the company to focus more on the hard disk drive-specific space, while the latter takes on more of the flash (NAND) chip space.

Conclusion

I'm expecting big things from the earnings report this week for WDC. There has been huge growth in earnings and margins in recent quarters as the firm picks up more long-term contracts from cloud providers. Revenue has been lagging in recent years as the price per TB for hard drives is near the bottom of its cycle. I anticipate a huge revenue rebound and a spark to what could be just the beginning of an already strong run. I predict exabytes shipped to have another strong showing and be near 230 for Q3. Look for management commentary on the rollout of their 40TB drive, as this could be the most efficient in the game over other peers if it is finalized this year.

Even though WDC has seen tremendous price return, I think we may look back at this period as an opportunity to buy this stock at a discount. My opinion is that we are entering a multiple-year structural upcycle that will come with big earnings and huge profit boosts. I think many things have aligned for a strong run for WDC. Value is only slightly inflated, earnings and demand are booming, and revenue is about to get the spark it needs as hard disk drive pricing begins its climb again. Buy WDC.

data.title: Net Lease Office Properties: A $21 Bear Case For A $13 Stock
data.description:
Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) is a special situation REIT in the middle of a portfolio liquidation. NLOP was spun off from W. P. Carey (WPC) in 2023 and has been steadily selling assets ever since, shrinking its portfolio from 59 to 18. This is the capital being returned to shareholders through the ~50%+ forward yield.

In order to understand if NLOP is still a buy, we need to ask, what are the remaining 18 properties worth?

Based on the current annualized base rent of ~$26M and reasonable market cap rates, these 18 properties appear to support a liquidation value in the $20-22/sh range, well above the current ~$13 price. This gap between the potential asset sale value and the market’s frankly pessimistic outlook creates a cautious buying opportunity.

An Overview of NLOP

NLOP's stated business plan is "realizing value for our shareholders primarily through strategic asset management and disposition of our property portfolio."

At its origin, it had 59 single-tenant office properties generating around $145M in annualized rent. Currently, only 18 properties are left, which yield ~$26M in rent—implying that the higher-value properties were skimmed off in the earlier transactions. Point to remember for valuation later: these 18 should not fetch the same cap rates as the other 41.

Anyway, proceeds from those sales were used to repay $455M of debt, and shareholders were paid multiple large special dividends. This gives us our framing—NLOP is a shrinking asset base being converted into cash, and traditional REIT metrics do not apply here. The only thing that matters is what price they can net for these properties and whether that is more than their current equity value.

The Remaining 18 Properties

These 18 properties have 79% occupancy (Dec 2025 figure) with a ~3.0-year weighted average lease term. That means this is not a table portfolio, partially vacant, shorter-duration, relatively speaking, and heavily dependent on individual tenants—not a chain. This directly impacts valuation.

A few characteristics define the remaining portfolio. First, tenants are somewhat concentrated, so a large portion of rent comes from a handful of them. A single lease event, like a renewal or a termination, can materially impact realized value.

Second, with WALT of only ~3.0 years, there are near-term leasing risks for many properties. This sort of uncertainty will be priced into acquisition cap rates by buyers.

Third, the vacancy is concentrated rather than broad-based. The remaining portfolio table shows two vacant former BCBSM assets in Eagan, MN, totaling 454,828 square feet, or about 24% of the remaining 1.875M square feet. That concentration helps explain the occupancy figure, but these vacant assets are still a real risk because their sale value may be harder to underwrite.

The key challenge is office space headwinds. These are primarily single-tenant office properties, and comprising nearly 40% of all distressed CRE assets, it is arguably the most challenging segment in commercial real estate today.

While the above discussion directionally explains some of the steep discount, it doesn’t justify that discount altogether. While these are non-core locations, occupancy is still solid—and so shouldn’t be considered for extreme distress pricing. To analyse that, we will convert the rental figure to a proper liquidation value using realistic cap rates and, critically, actual transaction evidence from prior asset sales.

Valuing the 18 Properties

Our investment case rests on converting the remaining $26M ABR into liquidation value. There are no recurring earnings to project, no growth to model. Those 18 properties represent the entire outcome.

The first step is to get a proper cap rate. Office real estate today can have multiple cap rates; high-quality, long-lease assets could be 7-9%, average office could be 9-11%, and short-lease, secondary, vacancy-risk assets could be 10-13%, depending on location and other factors.

Given NLOP’s remaining portfolio has 75-79% occupancy and 3-year WALT, with meaningful tenant concentration but multiple smaller/secondary assets. This is not prime pricing, but I would argue for a 10-12% cap rate, not over that. The bull case would go lower.

Now look at the valuation scenarios using a $26.3M ABR:

Add balance sheet—cash of ~120M and debt of ~$22M:

Using shares outstanding: ~14.8M, we get NAV per share:

Now we can test these assumptions against actual transactions. NLOP has already sold a majority of its portfolio, providing real pricing data.

The results are mixed. Several assets cleared the 8-11% range. That KBR transaction is a highly distressed outlier. However, the market seems to be effectively pricing most of the remaining assets at distressed cap rates, near the KBR figure. In fact, if you simply take the implied EV of $101M and the $26M ABR rate, you get a ~26% cap rate. This is not supported by the realized transactions so far, nor by the details of the 18 remaining properties.

So I will take a 10-12% cap rate here, and that implies a liquidation value meaningfully above the current share price.

At ~$13/share (~$195M market cap), removing cash and adding debt, we get implied remaining properties worth $101M, the enterprise value of the company. Even our bear case is more than that. There are only two ways to reconcile this gap. Either we say that the market is correct to expect significant deterioration before those assets are sold, or it is over-discounting execution risk.

This gap between the modeled value and the market price is where the opportunity (or trap if you are looking at it from another angle) lies. The remaining question is not what the assets are truly worth, but whether they can be sold at those levels. As anyone who has dabbled in real estate knows—like this author, for example—what a property is worth and what you can get for it are not always the same number.

Risks to a Buy Thesis

Several risks exist and may account for why the market is valuing NLOP as a distressed REIT asset seller.

The first is a simple execution risk. We are assuming that management will be able to sell the remaining assets at cap rates much below distressed rates. However, real estate sales are negotiated deals, and there’s no formula that can tell you where one might end up. If buyer demand weakens or management loses patience, realized price could fall short of our modeled values.

The second one is the short lease profile and two vacant assets. Those two, probably, are not going to fetch high premiums. The short lease profile will also be a hurdle. If more tenants depart before sales can be closed, that would reduce both income and valuation.

A third risk is that the bulk of the higher-quality assets have largely been sold. Their cap rates and sale values may not be appropriate metrics for the rest. Indeed, that could perhaps be the precise reason these are still not sold. Plus, the original portfolio generated ~$145M of rent across 59 properties. The remaining 18 properties generate only ~$26M, which means these are not as prime as the other 41.

A fourth risk is the tenant concentration. The top tenant alone accounts for $4.1M or ~15% of ABR, while the top 5 account for ~$16M+ (~60%+ of ABR). That means if these tenants leave, property value does not hold.

The overarching risk is, of course, the market condition of commercial real estate, where office real estate remains the most distressed. Taken together, these may justify part of the market reaction—especially as opposed to our bull case.

Conclusion

Given the above risk profile, I am not able to fully justify the market reaction vis-a-vis my bear case, which still gives me a share price of $21, nearly 60% above the current market price. While the market is surely pricing in all the risks I discussed, and possibly more besides, it does appear that the gap is too much. The company has managed to close many transactions within a closed cluster of 7-11% cap rate. There are 4-5 outliers that are obvious distress sales, but these are just that—outliers. Unless we consider all these 18 properties as outliers, a 10-12% cap rate is justifiable.

That is why I rate NLOP a cautious Buy at current levels.
